title: Cups to Grams Converter — All Baking Ingredients 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: 🥣 Convert any number of cups to grams for flour, sugar, butter, oil, milk, honey and 17+ ingredients. Uses USDA & King Arthur densities. 1 cup flour = 120 g,…
seoKeywords: how many grams in a cup
intro: The universal question in baking: **how many grams are in a cup?** The answer depends on the ingredient — a cup of flour weighs **120 g**, but a cup of butter weighs **227 g**, and a cup of honey a whopping **340 g**. This converter uses the reference densities published by **USDA FoodData Central** and **King Arthur Baking Company** for 17 common baking ingredients so your recipes come out right every time.
keyTakeaway: A **US cup** is **240 ml** (volume), but the **mass in grams depends on the ingredient's density**. All-purpose flour = **120 g/cup**, granulated sugar = **200 g/cup**, butter = **227 g/cup**, honey = **340 g/cup**. Always pick the ingredient from the dropdown — don't assume "1 cup = 240 g".
useCases: You're following a US recipe that uses cups and your kitchen only has a gram scale.
You want consistent results: baking is chemistry, and cups-by-eye vary ±20%.
You're scaling a recipe up or down and need precise quantities.
You're translating an American cookbook for metric kitchens.
You're a pastry student learning why 1 cup of flour ≠ 1 cup of sugar in weight.

fields.label: How many cups?
fields.help: US cup = 240 ml. Enter fractions as decimals (1/2 = 0.5, 1/4 = 0.25).

dataUpdate.source: USDA FoodData Central + King Arthur Baking Company reference weights
dataUpdate.notes: Densities are industry-standard. Volume standard: 1 US cup = 240 ml. For UK/metric cup (250 ml) multiply results by 1.042.

example.title: Example: 2 cups of all-purpose flour
example.result: **2 cups of all-purpose flour = 240 g** (about 8.5 oz, or 32 tablespoons).

explanation:
## Why 1 cup ≠ 1 cup (by weight)

A **cup** is a **volume** measurement — 240 ml in the US standard. But different ingredients have different **densities** (mass per unit volume), so the same cup holds different weights:

| Ingredient | 1 cup (grams) | Why |
|---|---:|---|
| **Rolled oats, cocoa** | 85 g | Low-density, fluffy |
| **Whole wheat flour** | 113 g | Slightly denser than white |
| **All-purpose flour** | 120 g | Industry standard |
| **Nuts (chopped)** | 115 g | Depends on size |
| **Chocolate chips** | 170 g | Dense chunks |
| **Granulated sugar** | 200 g | Dense crystals |
| **Brown sugar (packed)** | 213 g | Packed = more mass |
| **Vegetable oil** | 218 g | Liquid |
| **Butter** | 227 g | Dense solid fat |
| **Milk** | 245 g | Denser than water (solids) |
| **Honey** | 340 g | ~1.4× water density |

## Why US vs metric cups matter

- **US cup** = 240 ml (legal definition: 236.588 ml, rounded to 240 in recipes).
- **UK / Commonwealth cup** = 250 ml.
- **Japanese cup** = 200 ml.
- **Metric cup** (Australia) = 250 ml.

This calculator uses the **US cup (240 ml)**. If your recipe specifies a metric cup, multiply results by 1.042.

## How to measure cups correctly ("spoon and level")

For **flour** especially, how you fill the cup matters a lot. The reference 120 g/cup assumes the **spoon-and-level method**:

1. Fluff the flour with a spoon in the container (breaks up compaction).
2. Spoon flour into the measuring cup until it overflows.
3. Level off with a flat edge (knife back, ruler, etc.).

If you **scoop** directly with the measuring cup (pressing into the flour), you can pack **140–160 g/cup** — up to 30% more flour than the recipe assumes. That's the #1 reason home baking fails.

## Why bakers use grams

Professional and serious home bakers weigh ingredients instead of measuring volumes. Reasons:

- **Consistency**: 120 g is 120 g every time. 1 cup scooped varies ±20%.
- **Accuracy**: hydration ratios in bread (e.g. 65%) are weight-based, not volume.
- **Fewer dishes**: one bowl on a scale vs. multiple measuring cups.
- **Scaling**: halving or doubling is trivial with weights.

## Common recipe conversions (quick reference)

- **1/4 cup flour** ≈ 30 g
- **1/3 cup flour** ≈ 40 g
- **1/2 cup flour** ≈ 60 g
- **1 cup flour** = 120 g
- **2 cups flour** = 240 g
- **1 stick butter** (US) = 1/2 cup = 113 g
- **1 cup sugar** = 200 g
- **1 cup brown sugar packed** = 213 g

## Sources

- [USDA FoodData Central — Standard ingredient densities](https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/)
- [King Arthur Baking Company — Ingredient weight chart](https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/learn/ingredient-weight-chart)
- NIST Handbook 44 — Specifications, Tolerances for Weighing and Measuring Devices.

faq.a: It depends on what you're measuring. **1 cup of all-purpose flour = 120 g**, **1 cup of sugar = 200 g**, **1 cup of butter = 227 g**, **1 cup of honey = 340 g**. Use the dropdown to pick your ingredient.

faq.q: How many grams is 2 cups of flour?
faq.a: **240 g** of all-purpose flour (2 × 120). For bread flour it's 254 g, whole wheat 226 g, cake flour 228 g. The calculator distinguishes between them.

faq.q: Is a US cup the same as a metric cup?
faq.a: No. US cup = **240 ml**. Metric/UK cup = **250 ml** (about 4.2% larger). Japanese cup = 200 ml. Always check which standard your recipe uses. This converter uses the US cup.

faq.q: Why should I weigh instead of measure by cups?
faq.a: Because cups-by-volume vary depending on how you scoop. Flour can pack 20–30% more if you press the measuring cup into the bag vs. spoon-and-level. Grams give you **consistent results** every time.

faq.q: How do I measure brown sugar: packed or loose?
faq.a: **Packed**. Recipes assume brown sugar is pressed firmly into the cup until it holds its shape when turned out. That's the 213 g/cup figure. Loose brown sugar is roughly 160 g/cup — 25% less — and will throw off your recipe.
Halve any 1-cup number. **1/2 cup flour = 60 g**, **1/2 cup sugar = 100 g**, **1/2 cup butter = 113 g** (that's one US "stick"), **1/2 cup oil = 109 g**.

faq.q: Does "1 cup" on a US stick of butter match the calculator?
faq.a: A full US stick is **1/2 cup = 113 g**. Two sticks = 1 cup = **227 g**. The calculator uses 227 g/cup for butter, consistent with most US packaging.
Yes — 100% free, no signup, no ads in your result. Just enter the number of cups and pick the ingredient.

sources.name: King Arthur Baking Company — Ingredient weight chart

howToSteps.name: Enter how many cups
howToSteps.text: Type a number of cups (whole or decimal). 1/2 = 0.5, 1/4 = 0.25, 3/4 = 0.75.

howToSteps.name: Pick your ingredient
howToSteps.text: Select from the dropdown — the density changes dramatically between flour, sugar, butter, honey, etc.

howToSteps.name: Read the weight
howToSteps.text: The primary result is the weight in grams. You also get ounces (US) and tablespoons for cross-reference.
